A: The university name (as a College) was officially changed to Virginia Commonwealth University in 1968, and previously was known as the Richmond Professional Institute in 1917 and before that the Medical College of Virginia in 1838.
A: Exit? No.
A: ["2015\u201316 VCU Rams men's basketball team", "2011\u201312 VCU Rams men's basketball team", "Virginia Commonwealth University", "Fujian Medical University"]
A: The university name (as a College) was officially changed to Virginia Commonwealth University in 1968, and previously was known as the Richmond Professional Institute in 1917 and before that the Medical College of Virginia in 1838. However it all started as the Medical department of Hampden–Sydney College, which is a separate institution, founded in 1838.

A: The university name (as a College) was officially changed to Virginia Commonwealth University in 1968, and previously was known as the Richmond Professional Institute in 1917 and before that the Medical College of Virginia in 1838. A: Paul Sauvage did not fly L'Oiseau Blanc. Raymond Collin and Henri Lemaire, French pilot and adventurer, were the first men to fly from Paris to Brazil by flying in a plane called "L'Oiseau Blanc" and crashed in the British Guiana (now Guyana). Charles Nungesser did fly L'Oiseau Blanc along with François Coli from Paris to New York. They crashed, or crashed and died, during the flight. Charles Nungesser is the French pilot who flew along with François Coli in the doomed L'Oiseau Blanc from Paris to New York. The first men to fly L'Oiseau Blanc were indeed: Paul "Lestang" Lescop along with Georges Le Bruns pilot Raymond Collin and Henri Lemaire French pilot and adventurer to fly from Paris to Brazil in 1919 on the plane called L'Oiseau Blanc. François Coli and Charles Nungesser were the crew that the plane was sold to, later, and were the ones that attempted a flight of L'Oiseau Blanc in 1927,  flying from Paris to New York to win the Orteig Prize. These are the correct correct pilot: Henri Lemaire, Raymond Collin, Paul Lestang, Georges Le Bruns, François Coli and Charles Nungesser. .Paul

Q: Alexander Kerensky was defeated and destroyed by the Bolsheviks in the course of a civil war that ended when ?
A: "Kerensky\u2013Krasnov uprising - a Russian Army soldiers riot broke out in Moscow and was helped by the Czech Legion it ended when the Czech Legion, which had originally been created to continue fighting Germany in World War I, decided to stop fighting the Bolsheviks and begin their own military campaign against them.  They would end their campaign and flee Russia once the Czech Legion became friendly and promised them passage out of Russia."
A: Kerensky\u2013Krasnov uprising - a Russian Army soldiers riot broke out in Moscow and was helped by the Czech Legion it ended when the Czech Legion, which had originally been created to continue fighting Germany in World War I, decided to stop fighting the Bolsheviks and begin their own military campaign against them.  They would end their campaign and flee Russia once the Czech Legion became friendly and promised them passage out of Russia

A: Exit? No.
A: ["Livesey Hall War Memorial", "Masni\u00e8res Newfoundland Memorial", "Rainham War Memorial", "World War I"]
A: The Livesey Hal War Memorial commemorates the fallen of World War I and World War II.   World War I is often referred to as one of the deadliest conflict in all history, with over 37 million casualties, including 17 million deaths. However, it is also possible that the Memorial refers to World War I casualty rate being the casualty rate exacerbated by the belligerents' technological and industrial sophistication, and the tactical stalemate caused by gruelling trench warfare, and the casualties including the victims of a number of genocides.

A: ["Livesey Hall War Memorial", "Masni\u00e8res Newfoundland Memorial", "Rainham War Memorial", "World War I"]
A: The Livesey Hal War Memorial commemorates the fallen of World War I and World War II.   World War I is often referred to as one of the deadliest conflict in all history, with over 37 million casualties, including 17 million deaths. However, it is also possible that the Memorial refers to World War I casualty rate being the casualty rate exacerbated by the belligerents' technological and industrial sophistication, and the tactical stalemate caused by gruelling trench warfare, and the casualties including the victims of a number of genocides. The reference to 60 million was made in the context of the total number of military personnel, including 60 million Europeans, that were mobilised in World War I, not only the casualties.

Q: The battle in which Giuseppe Arimondi lost his life secured what for Ethiopia?
A: "The country's independence.  In 1941 British troops led by General Archibald Wavell defeated the Italian army in the Second Battle of Keren, which led to the Italian army's withdrawal from the Horn of Africa and ultimately the end of the Italian occupation of Ethiopia in May 1941.  Giuseppe Arimondi was an Italian officer killed in action during this battle."
A: The country's independence.  In 1941 British troops led by General Archibald Wavell defeated the Italian army in the Second Battle of Keren, which led to the Italian army's withdrawal from the Horn of Africa and ultimately the end of the Italian occupation of Ethiopia in May 1941.  Giuseppe Arimondi was an Italian officer killed in action during this battle

Q: A medieval fortress in Dirleton, East Lothian, Scotland borders on the south side of what coastal area?
A: "The answer is: the village of Dirleton borders on the south side of the Yellowcraigs, less commonly known as Broad Sands Bay. Yellowcraigs is a coastal area of forest, beach and grassland in East Lothian, south-east Scotland.  It is bordered to the north by the Firth of Forth, to the south by the village of Dirleton and Dirleton Castle, to the east by the North Berwick West Links golf course, and to the west"
A: the village of Dirleton borders on the south side of the Yellowcraigs, less commonly known as Broad Sands Bay. Yellowcraigs is a coastal area of forest, beach and grassland in East Lothian, south-east Scotland.  It is bordered to the north by the Firth of Forth, to the south by the village of Dirleton and Dirleton Castle, to the east by the North Berwick West Links golf course, and to the west

She appeared in a number of B Movies in a mixture of leading and supporting roles.   She starred in the 1945 film noir "Detour". A woman starred in Johnny Angel in 1945 and her name is the subject of the Wikipedia title "Vera Ralston".   Vera Ralston (7 April 1919 – 9 September 2003) was a Slovak-born American actress and figure skater.   She starred in Western and melodrama films, often under contract to Republic Pictures.    Ralston's most notable role was in Johnny Angel (1945), in which she starred as a carnival performer. Vera Ralston has been featured on the cover of the classic paperback novel "Men, Women and Dogs" published in 1954 by Martin Kane. After leaving the film industry in 1953, Ralston lived a quiet life with her second husband, the businessman and art collector Alfred Tate.

A: Exit? No.
A: ["514th Flight Test Squadron", "370th Flight Test Squadron", "415th Flight Test Flight", "419th Flight Test Squadron"]
A: There is no mention in the given information that 514th Flight Test Squadron is stationed at Hill Air Force Base in honour of some Major. We are given information that it is assigned to the Ogden Air Logistics Center (OO-ALC), Air Force Materiel Command. In fact, Hill Air Force Base is named after Maj. Ployer P. Hill who commanded the 308th Bomb Group.
A: Exit? No.
A: ["514th Flight Test Squadron", "370th Flight Test Squadron", "415th Flight Test Flight", "419th Flight Test Squadron", "Hill Air Force Base", "Schilling Air Force Base"]
A: There is no mention in the given information that 514th Flight Test Squadron is stationed at Hill Air Force Base in honour of some Major. We are given information that it is assigned to the Ogden Air Logistics Center (OO-ALC), Air Force Materiel Command. In fact, Hill Air Force Base is named after Maj. Ployer P. Hill who commanded the 308th Bomb Group. The base is named after Maj. Hill because he was test-flying a B-17 bomber at that base when he died in the crash of his aircraft.
A: Exit? No.
A: ["514th Flight Test Squadron", "370th Flight Test Squadron", "415th Flight Test Flight", "419th Flight Test Squadron", "Hill Air Force Base", "Schilling Air Force Base", "Edwin Andrews Air Base"]
A: There is no mention in the given information that 514th Flight Test Squadron is stationed at Hill Air Force Base in honour of some Major. We are given information that it is assigned to the Ogden Air Logistics Center (OO-ALC), Air Force Materiel Command. In fact, Hill Air Force Base is named after Maj. Ployer P. Hill who commanded the 308th Bomb Group. The base is named after Maj. Hill because he was test-flying a B-17 bomber at that base when he died in the crash of his aircraft. Thus, the answer is: B-17.

Q: Which airplane was this Major test-flying after whom the base, that 514th Flight Test Squadron is stated at, is named?
A: "The base is named in honor of Major Ployer Peter Hill of the U.S. Army Air Corps. He died test-flying a prototype of the B-17 Flying Fortress bomber. So the answer is: B-17 Flying Fortress. The Discovery Institute is a politically conservative non-profit think tank based in Seattle, Washington, best known for its advocacy of the pseudoscientific principle of intelligent design (ID). Q: The Kansas City Wizards have had three home venues in Kansas City in their time.  Which was their first?
A: "The Kansas City Wizards have had three home venues in Kansas City in their time.  Their home venues have been: The Home Depot Center (used only once for the 2004 MLS Cup), Community America Ballpark, and lastly Arrowhead Stadium. Their first home venue used for the 2008 season was Community America Ballpark. So the answer is: Community America Ballpark."

Q: Are Hot Rod and The Memory of Our People both magazines?
A: "The answer is no, they are not both magazines. They have the same suffix (-Of or -Of Our), which might make them seem like both are magazines.  The Memory of Our People is a magazine, but Hot Rod is not a magazine about a gathering of nostalgia drag racers. Hot Rod is a different term. Hot Rod is a noun, and its plural is Hot Rods. Weekly Shonen Jump is a digital "shōnen" manga anthology published in North America by Viz Media, and the successor to their monthly print anthology "Shonen Jump".   It began serialization on January 30, 2012 as Weekly Shonen Jump Alpha (officially stylized as "Weekly SHONEN JUMP αlpha" or "Weekly SHONEN JUMP Alpha"), with two free preview issues released in the buildup to its launch. Based on Shueisha's popular Japanese magazine "Weekly Shōnen Jump", "Weekly Shonen Jump" is an attempt to provide English readers with easily accessible, affordable, and officially licensed editions of the latest installments of popular "Shōnen Jump" manga soon after their release in Japan, as an alternative to popular bootleg scanlation services.

Weekly Shonen Jump is a digital "shōnen" manga anthology published in North America by Viz Media, and the successor to their monthly print anthology "Shonen Jump".   It began serialization on January 30, 2012 as Weekly Shonen Jump Alpha (officially stylized as "Weekly SHONEN JUMP αlpha" or "Weekly SHONEN JUMP Alpha"), with two free preview issues released in the buildup to its launch. Based on Shueisha's popular Japanese magazine "Weekly Shōnen Jump", "Weekly Shonen Jump" is an attempt to provide English readers with easily accessible, affordable, and officially licensed editions of the latest installments of popular "Shōnen Jump" manga soon after their release in Japan, as an alternative to popular bootleg scanlation services. So Shonen Jump is published more often, at least until 2012 when Weekly Shonen Jump started being published.

Q: Which magazine is published more often, Shonen Jump or Rolling Stone?
A: "The two magazines have different publishing frequencies. Shonen Jump is a monthly magazine while Rolling Stone is a monthly magazine as well but, whereas Shonen Jump originally published in a Weekly format in Japan and Weekly Shonen Jump Alpha was an additional short period of Weekly format publication in the US, originally \"Shonen Jump\" monthly in the US was once a Monthly and then changed to being a Bimonthly before reverting to Monthly. Rolling Stone has also gone through different formats but the question isn't"
A: The two magazines have different publishing frequencies. Shonen Jump is a monthly magazine while Rolling Stone is a monthly magazine as well but, whereas Shonen Jump originally published in a Weekly format in Japan and Weekly Shonen Jump Alpha was an additional short period of Weekly format publication in the US, originally \"Shonen Jump\" monthly in the US was once a Monthly and then changed to being a Bimonthly before reverting to Monthly. Rolling Stone has also gone through different formats but the question isn't
